subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Subsidiarity, Sincere Cooperation, and the Role of General Principles

questions.question: The yellow card procedure under Protocol 2 on Subsidiarity allows:

options.A: The Commission to pause a legislative proposal when a majority of member state governments objects
options.B: If one-third or more of national parliaments issue reasoned opinions within eight weeks, the Commission must review the proposal and decide whether to maintain, amend, or withdraw it
options.C: The European Council to veto a Commission legislative proposal by simple majority
options.D: Any single national parliament to delay implementation of a Directive for six months

questions.explanation: Protocol 2: the yellow card requires one-third of votes allocated to national parliaments (or one-quarter for the area of freedom, security, and justice) to issue reasoned opinions within eight weeks. The Commission must review and provide reasons for its decision to maintain, amend, or withdraw. However, the Commission is not legally required to withdraw — it can maintain the proposal with reasons.

questions.question: The orange card procedure under Protocol 2 applies when:

options.A: The European Parliament rejects a Commission proposal on subsidiarity grounds
options.B: A simple majority of national parliament votes issues reasoned opinions — the Commission reviews, and if it maintains the proposal, the Council or Parliament may reject it on subsidiarity grounds by a 55% majority or majority vote respectively
options.C: A two-thirds majority of national parliaments issues objections, requiring an automatic withdrawal
options.D: The Court of Auditors issues a negative subsidiarity assessment of a proposed measure

questions.explanation: The orange card (for Justice and Home Affairs matters specifically) requires a simple majority threshold of national parliament votes issuing reasoned opinions. If the Commission maintains the proposal, the Council (55% of members) or Parliament (simple majority) may reject it for subsidiarity reasons. The orange card thus creates a potential legislative veto on subsidiarity grounds in JHA.

questions.question: The sincere cooperation principle (Article 4(3) TEU) was used by the Court of Justice to develop:

options.A: The principle of qualified majority voting in the Council
options.B: State liability in damages for breach of EU law — the Court derived member states' obligation to provide effective remedies partly from sincere cooperation
options.C: The doctrine of direct effect of Treaty provisions
options.D: The exclusivity of EU competence in the customs union

questions.explanation: In Francovich [1991], the Court derived the obligation on member states to compensate individuals for loss caused by breaches of EU law partly from the principle of sincere cooperation (then Article 5 EEC, now Article 4(3) TEU) — member states must take all appropriate measures to fulfil EU obligations, including providing effective remedies for individuals harmed by their breaches. Combined with effectiveness, this generated state liability.

questions.question: The role of general principles of EU law in the EU legal order is to:

options.A: Replace written Treaty provisions wherever they are ambiguous
options.B: Fill gaps in the written law, serve as standards for review of EU acts, inform interpretation of EU and national law, and create directly enforceable rights
options.C: Provide guidelines for the Commission when exercising its discretion, without binding force on member states
options.D: Operate exclusively at the international law level, without domestic effect

questions.explanation: General principles of EU law perform multiple functions: they fill gaps where the Treaties and secondary law are silent; they provide standards against which the Court reviews EU acts (disproportionate or contrary to fundamental rights → annulment); they guide interpretation; and, as Mangold showed, they can generate directly enforceable individual rights overriding inconsistent national law.

questions.question: The principle of proportionality under Article 5(4) TEU requires not only that EU action be proportionate (institutional proportionality) but also what additional element in the context of competence?

options.A: That EU action is always less extensive than equivalent member state action
options.B: That the content and form of Union action shall not exceed what is necessary to achieve Treaty objectives — it applies as a control on how the EU uses its competences
options.C: That member states' financial contributions to the EU budget are proportionate to their GDP
options.D: That the Commission must demonstrate that at least three alternative less-restrictive measures were considered before proposing legislation

questions.explanation: Article 5(4) TEU: proportionality applies to the content and form of Union action, requiring it not to exceed what is necessary to achieve Treaty objectives. Combined with conferral (the EU can only act within its competences) and subsidiarity (the EU should act only where member states cannot achieve the objective), proportionality forms the third leg of the competence control framework.

questions.question: The duty of consistent interpretation (indirect effect) derives principally from which principle?

options.B: The principle of sincere cooperation (Article 4(3) TEU) — national courts must interpret national law in conformity with EU law to the greatest extent possible
options.C: The principle of supremacy — inconsistent national law must be reinterpreted rather than disapplied
options.D: The Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 47 — effective judicial protection requires conforming interpretation

questions.explanation: In Von Colson [1984] and Marleasing [1990], the Court derived the duty of consistent/conforming interpretation from the principle of sincere cooperation (formerly Article 5 EEC): since member states must take all measures to ensure fulfilment of EU obligations, this includes the obligation on national courts to interpret national law in light of EU Directives' aims. The duty applies to all national law, not just implementing legislation.

questions.question: The general principle of effectiveness requires that:

options.A: EU law must have direct effect in all member states
options.B: National procedural rules governing enforcement of EU rights must not make it practically impossible or excessively difficult to exercise those rights
options.C: The Commission must take enforcement action against member states that fail to implement Directives within six months
options.D: National courts must award the same remedies for EU law breaches as for equivalent domestic law claims

questions.explanation: The effectiveness principle (developed from Rewe v Landwirtschaftskammer [1976] and Comet [1976]) limits national procedural autonomy: member states may choose their own procedural rules for EU law claims, but those rules must not make exercise of EU rights excessively difficult or practically impossible. Combined with the equivalence principle (no less favourable treatment than equivalent national claims), it forms the twin constraints on national procedural autonomy.

questions.question: General principles of EU law may be derived from:

options.A: Regulations adopted by the Commission under delegated powers
options.B: Constitutional traditions common to member states, the European Convention on Human Rights, and the EU's own legal requirements
options.C: Decisions of the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg, which are binding on the CJEU
options.D: Unanimous agreement of the European Council on fundamental legal values

questions.explanation: The Court of Justice has derived general principles from: the constitutional traditions common to member states (e.g., rights of defence, legal professional privilege); the ECHR (as a source of inspiration, not binding law — Article 6(3) TEU); and from the nature of the EU legal order itself (supremacy, direct effect, procedural principles). These are then developed into EU general principles binding on EU institutions and member states.

questions.question: The principle of conferral, subsidiarity, and proportionality together constitute:

options.A: The institutional triangle of EU governance
options.B: The three principles governing the exercise of EU competences — conferral governs which competences the EU has; subsidiarity governs when to exercise them; proportionality governs how to exercise them
options.C: The three grounds for annulment of EU acts under Article 263 TFEU
options.D: The three pillars of the post-Lisbon EU constitutional framework

questions.explanation: Article 5 TEU structures all three as competence principles: conferral (Article 5(1) and 5(2)) — the EU acts only within competences conferred by the member states; subsidiarity (Article 5(3)) — in non-exclusive competences, the EU acts only where objectives cannot be sufficiently achieved by member states; proportionality (Article 5(4)) — EU action does not exceed what is necessary to achieve Treaty objectives.

questions.question: The Court of Justice's approach to developing general principles from 'constitutional traditions common to member states' involves:

options.A: Applying the rule of the member state with the highest standard to all EU situations
options.B: Identifying shared core principles across member state legal systems and developing them as EU principles, without necessarily importing any single member state's rule wholesale
options.C: Conducting a survey of member state constitutions and adopting the majority position
options.D: Deferring to the member state most directly affected by the question to determine the applicable principle

questions.explanation: The Court does not mechanically apply comparative law but identifies underlying shared constitutional values and adapts them to the specific requirements of the EU legal order. As the Court noted in Nold [1974], fundamental rights 'form an integral part of the general principles of Community law' and the Court draws inspiration from constitutional traditions 'common' to member states — common principles, not necessarily identical rules.

questions.question: The principle of procedural autonomy means that, in the absence of EU rules:

options.A: Member states have no obligation to provide remedies for EU law claims
options.B: Member states may apply their own procedural rules to EU law claims, subject to the twin requirements of equivalence and effectiveness
options.C: EU institutions directly administer EU law claims in member states
options.D: National courts must apply EU procedural law derived from the TFEU

questions.explanation: Procedural autonomy: it is for member states to lay down the detailed procedural rules governing actions intended to safeguard rights derived from EU law (Rewe; Comet). However, such rules are subject to: (1) equivalence — must not be less favourable than those governing similar domestic actions; (2) effectiveness — must not render exercise of EU rights practically impossible or excessively difficult.

questions.question: The right of access to a lawyer and legal professional privilege as a general principle of EU law was established in:

options.A: AM & S Europe v Commission [1982] — communications between a client and independent lawyer made for the purposes and in the interests of the client's rights of defence are privileged from Commission inspection
options.B: Hoechst v Commission [1989] — all documents in an undertaking's possession are exempt from Commission searches
options.C: Roquette Frères v Directeur Général [2002] — internal company documents are automatically protected from EU competition authority investigation
options.D: Ordre des Barreaux Francophones v Conseil des Ministres [2007] — all legal advice is protected regardless of who provides it

questions.explanation: In AM & S Europe (Case 155/79), the Court recognised legal professional privilege as a general principle of EU law derived from the constitutional traditions of member states. Privileged communications are: (1) between a client and independent (external) lawyer; (2) made for the purposes of defence rights; (3) emanating from independent lawyers authorised to practise in an EU member state. In-house lawyers' communications are NOT privileged (Akzo Nobel v Commission [2010]).

questions.question: The principle of equivalence in EU remedies law requires:

options.A: All EU law claims to be decided by the same court regardless of the claim type
options.B: National procedural rules applicable to EU law claims must be no less favourable than those applicable to similar domestic law claims
options.C: All member states must award equivalent compensation for breaches of EU law
options.D: The Commission must provide equivalent funding to all member states to support EU law enforcement

questions.explanation: The equivalence principle (Rewe; Comet; later Levez v Jennings) requires that the conditions for bringing an EU law claim must be no less favourable than those applicable to similar actions under national law. National time limits, cost rules, and procedural requirements must not disadvantage EU law claims compared to equivalent domestic claims. This prevents procedural discrimination against EU rights.
